https://www.evidenza.ai/research/can-ai-.description: Many chief marketing officers want their market research to possess more of these three qualities: Faster, cheaper, better. https://www.evidenza.ai/research/imperso.title: Impersonalization at Scale
https://www.evidenza.ai/research/imperso.description: What’s the worst idea in the marketing industry? Everybody has their own answer to that question. For some, it’s brand purpose. For others, it’s short-termism. For us, the answer is clear: personalization. You can’t go more than five seconds at a marketing conference without hearing about the promise of “one-to-one personalization at scale”. Personalization continues to be one of the biggest trends in the marketing industry. In 2019, it was named ‘word of the year’.

https://www.evidenza.ai/research/origina.title: The Originality Delusion
https://www.evidenza.ai/research/origina.description: Marketers have an obsession. A deep, dark, self-destructive obsession. What is the object of this obsession, you ask? In a word: newness. Marketers are absolutely obsessed with new things. New platforms. New products. And, above all else, new ideas. If I had a nickel for every time a client asked me for a never-been-done-before “big idea,” I could buy a one-bedroom apartment in Manhattan.

https://www.evidenza.ai/research/perform.title: Performance Branding
https://www.evidenza.ai/research/perform.description: Lead generation marketers have dominated the B2B marketing landscape over the last decade, in large part, by focusing on generating short-term results – sales leads —and positioning marketing as a precise science where all waste can be eliminated and all actions can be tied to a dollar outcome. By positioning brand marketers as wasteful, lead generation marketers have wrestled away control of marketing budgets and wrestled away credit for sales growth. However, we’d argue that lead generation is much less the science it claims to be and, instead, is much more the art it claims to replace. In particular, we bristle at the idea that lead generation produces more sales for companies than brand marketing. The marketing science makes clear that lead generation captures demand but doesn’t really create demand.
